title: Ambiguous destructive action copy vs destructive action confirmation vs typed confirmation vs dangerous-action review vs confirmation fatigue vs warning text
summary: Flag ambiguous destructive action copy when labels hide what will be destroyed, preserved, cancelled, revoked, or executed. Use the related patterns when the issue is the review surface, exact target gate, high-impact payload review, prompt overuse, or before-action warning copy.
decisionRules: Choose ambiguous destructive action copy when a destructive or safe control says OK, Yes, No, Continue, Confirm, Submit, Done, Remove, Cancel, Clear, Reset, Close, or Dismiss without naming the actual outcome and target.

Choose destructive action confirmation when the user needs a final destructive review with loss inventory, target scope, dependency blocking, acknowledgement, safe cancellation, and committed outcome states.

Choose typed confirmation when severe wrong-target risk requires users to reproduce a visible target phrase before the final action enables; the final typed-confirmation button still needs specific destructive copy.

Choose dangerous-action review when the risky decision is high-impact execution involving money, access, production, legal, customers, external recipients, sensitive data, safety, or agent tool use, and the user must inspect payload, evidence, freshness, and side effects.
Choose confirmation fatigue when the main defect is repeated low-value prompts, same-copy prompt spam, or confirmation habituation across routine actions.
Choose warning text when consequence copy should appear before users start the risky action, not as the final decision copy inside the prompt.
Use verb-plus-target final labels for destructive commits, such as Delete project, Revoke editor access, Cancel subscription, Permanently delete 12 files, or Run production restart.
Use preserved-outcome safe labels, such as Keep project, Keep subscription, Keep access, Cancel deletion, Close review, or Back to settings.
If Cancel can mean both close this prompt and cancel the underlying service, avoid it or qualify it with the object and outcome.
If Remove can mean detach, hide, unshare, revoke, move to trash, or delete permanently, name the exact operation instead of relying on context or color.
If a compact mobile label must be short, nearby text and accessible names must still include the destructive verb, target, and count.
Do not rely on red styling, button order, warning icon, or an earlier paragraph to make an OK or Continue button safe.
failureModes: A delete prompt uses OK and Cancel, so users cannot tell which button deletes the object when reading controls out of context.
A plan cancellation dialog uses Cancel for both closing the dialog and cancelling the subscription.
A permission dialog says Remove without distinguishing Revoke Maya's access from removing a row from the visible list.
A high-impact review uses Continue for actual execution, so users think they are moving to another preview step.
A bulk destructive action omits selected count and object type, hiding whether one item or many items will be deleted.
A screen reader exposes only Confirm because the specific destructive wording appears only in surrounding text.

sources.note: Apple alert guidance supports clear action wording and careful treatment of destructive choices.
Microsoft confirmation guidance supports specific consequences and avoiding unnecessary or vague confirmations.
Atlassian danger modal examples support destructive copy with affected content and cancellation.
Dialog guidance supports deliberate destructive confirmation rather than vague modal decisions.
Warning text guidance supports consequence copy before action when a modal decision is not the right surface.
